description: How societies organize power, make collective decisions, codify rules, and resolve disputes.

sectors.name: Political Systems & Institutions
sectors.description: Government structures, electoral systems, separation of powers, federalism, comparative politics

sectors.name: Legislation & Policy Process
sectors.description: How laws are made, regulatory rulemaking, administrative law, lobbying, policy evaluation

sectors.name: Judicial Systems & Legal Process
sectors.description: Court structures, civil and criminal procedure, evidence, judicial review, access to justice

sectors.name: Substantive Law
sectors.description: Contract, tort, property, criminal, constitutional rights, family law — US federal and common law

sectors.name: Intellectual Property
sectors.description: Patents, copyright, trademark, trade secrets, licensing, IP enforcement

sectors.name: International Relations & Law
sectors.description: Treaty law, diplomatic relations, international organizations, human rights law, laws of war, ICJ

sectors.name: Security & Conflict
sectors.description: Defense policy, intelligence oversight, conflict types, deterrence, arms control, cybersecurity law

sectors.name: Electoral Systems & Democracy
sectors.description: Voting systems, electoral integrity, campaign finance, democratic backsliding, representation

sectors.name: Geopolitical Analysis
sectors.description: Strategic competition, alliance systems, balance of power, regional dynamics, great power rivalry

fragment_rules.philosophy_guard: Every fragment must describe what is — not advocate for what should be. Descriptive claims about institutions, outcomes, and processes are fragmentable. Normative claims about what is right or wrong are not.
